title: Best Structured-Output LLM: Compare Prices Grid
description: Picking an LLM for structured-output workloads (JSON-mode, schema-constrained) via TokenRate's Compare Prices grid.

sections.heading: Structured-Output Generation: The Right Question Isn't "Which Model"
sections.body: TokenRate's new [Compare Prices grid](/tools/compare-prices) puts every model's per-token rates, context window, and quality score in a single side-by-side view. The point: stop flipping between provider pricing pages and OpenRouter tabs. You pick a provider dropdown, check the models you want, repeat for each provider, and the grid stacks every pick into one comparison table. For Structured-Output Generation the picking decision usually defaults to "use the model my last project used" — which is roughly the worst possible heuristic in 2026, because the price-quality frontier has shifted three times in the past year. The right question is "which combination of price tier, context length, and quality score fits Structured-Output Generation traffic patterns?" The [Compare Prices grid](/tools/compare-prices) is built for that question. This guide walks through three picks for Structured-Output Generation and shows how to grid them. See also: [filter LLM models by tier, cost, quality](/blog/filter-llm-models-by-tier-cost-quality), [Value column vs tokens-per-dollar](/blog/value-column-vs-tokens-per-dollar-metric), and [how to pick an LLM by quality score and cost](/blog/how-to-pick-llm-by-quality-score-and-cost).

sections.heading: The Workload Profile of Structured-Output Generation
sections.body: Structured-Output Generation workloads have a few distinguishing characteristics: output is JSON or schema-constrained; quality is measured by valid-JSON rate and schema-conformance rate; format determinism matters more than fluency. That profile tells you which columns of the [Compare Prices grid](/tools/compare-prices) matter most. Quality (specifically structured-output benchmarks), output cost (JSON is compact but matters at volume), input cost (schema in prompt). It also tells you which tier you should be in. For most Structured-Output Generation traffic, the [Balanced tier](/blog/flagship-balanced-fast-reasoning-llm-tiers) is the production default — quality high enough to ship to real users at scale, price low enough to make the unit economics work.

sections.heading: Top Pick: Claude Sonnet 4.7
sections.body: For Structured-Output Generation, Claude Sonnet 4.7 is the default candidate. Pricing: $3.00 input / $15.00 output per 1M tokens. Context: 200K. Quality score: 80. Tier: balanced. Why it wins: high structured-output reliability at a price point where production traffic is profitable. Add it to the [Compare Prices grid](/tools/compare-prices) and the Value column makes the case visually (26.7 quality per dollar of input cost). Where it loses: on edge schemas where Opus 4 or o3 may produce better adherence.

sections.heading: Runner-Ups and When to Pick Them
sections.body: **GPT-5** ($1.25 / $10.00, Q82) — pick this when quality is non-negotiable and the bill is a rounding error against the value of correct output. **GPT-5 mini** ($0.300 / $2.40, Q70) — pick this when you want the production-default balance of quality (70) and price ($0.300 input). All three live in the same [Compare Prices view](/tools/compare-prices) so the comparison is one screen, not three browser tabs. For workload-specific cost modeling, run your token volume through [/tools/api-cost-estimator](/tools/api-cost-estimator).

sections.heading: Compare-Prices Workflow for Structured-Output Generation
sections.body: Workflow: (1) open [/tools/compare-prices](/tools/compare-prices), (2) check the three picks across their provider dropdowns, (3) sort the resulting grid by Value column, (4) shortlist the top 1-2, (5) run an A/B against your real Structured-Output Generation traffic for a week. The shortlisting step is where 90% of the time savings happen — the grid eliminates obvious losers (low quality, wrong context, output-cost surprises) in seconds. The grid pulls prices live from [OpenRouter](https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models) and quality from a blended Arena AI + Artificial Analysis pipeline — both refresh on a 60-minute incremental cache, so the comparison reflects current rates not a baked-in snapshot. Open [/tools/compare-prices](/tools/compare-prices) now, pick your provider dropdowns, and pin the shortlist that matches your workload.

faq.question: How do I open the Compare Prices grid?
faq.answer: Two ways: click the 'Compare Prices' tab at the top of the calculator card on the home page, or navigate directly to /tools/compare-prices. The standalone page is also linked from the main navigation under 'Tools'.

faq.question: Can I share my comparison with teammates?
faq.answer: Yes — the page URL captures the current state. Send the link in Slack and your teammate sees the same grid. Useful for procurement and architecture-review meetings.

faq.question: Is the data live or cached?
faq.answer: Live from OpenRouter (prices) and a blended Arena AI + Artificial Analysis pipeline (quality), refreshed on a 60-minute incremental cache. So the grid is at most an hour stale.

faq.question: Where do I go after the grid to project monthly cost?
faq.answer: Once you've picked a winner, go to /tools/api-cost-estimator and plug in the model + your expected monthly token volume. The estimator does the per-1M math against your real workload mix.

ctaText: Open [/tools/compare-prices](/tools/compare-prices) now, pick your provider dropdowns, and pin the shortlist that matches your workload.
